i-5CX0qx0-ix.name: Web design principles
i-5CX0qx0-ix.description: These principles guide decision-making across marketing, web, and brand experiences. They provide a shared framework for clarity, coherence, and long-term maintainability of Canonical sites.

i-DlY4iCeiNo.description: Codification of our brand guidelines ensuring proper Canonical branding on Sites.

i-MughqPHCHW.name: Color
i-MughqPHCHW.description: Sites are using a predefined set of colors from the Design System palette. Color layering is also following a set of functional rules to ensure we apply the same visual choices across all our pages.

i-KFif7gGk3u.name: Spacing
i-KFif7gGk3u.description: Sites are using predefined vertical spacing tokens ensuring content is properly positioned and maintains vertical consistency.

i-T9g6Adkwpp.name: Intentional friction
i-T9g6Adkwpp.description:
Design friction is the intentional use of obstacles in a user interface to prevent accidental or destructive mistakes. It should be used when a user is about to perform an action that is irreversible, such as deleting data or changing permanent system settings.

For high-risk tasks, use blocking friction like requiring the user to type a confirmation word or check a box acknowledging specific risks. For medium-risk or advanced settings, use non-blocking friction like warning dialogs or technical language to ensure the user is acting deliberately rather than clicking through mindlessly.
i-T9g6Adkwpp.content:
It is sometimes desirable to introduce some friction in flows so users are fully aware of risks and take any irreversible or destructive actions knowingly. This friction is not supposed to be a substantial nuisance that keeps the user from acting deliberately, but instead a guardrail to prevent accidental interactions.

​For instance, a user using a wizard may be tempted to click repeatedly on the “Next” button. This may be OK in many cases. For instance, the defaults for the Ubuntu installer may work well enough when setting up a virtual machine. However, you may decide to disable that “Next” button in critical steps to prevent the user from taking destructive actions (like wiping a drive or not saving their recovery key) without explicitly acknowledging the risks (losing their data).

### Blocking friction

Adding friction that blocks the user from continuing the flow unless taking a very deliberate action is ideal for **destructive** actions.

For destructive actions, you may:

*   **Ask the user to type a word:** you may ask the user to type either the name of the action (e.g. “delete”) or the name of the object that will be destroyed (e.g. a VM or repository name).

  

![image.png](images/89d4082e-1330-4196-8a07-5b24bf7a183b.png)

​

For _potentially_ destructive actions, you may:

*   **Ask the user to acknowledge risk**: similar to the way we collect legal consent, it should be voiced in first tense, e.g. “I understand the risk”, ideally explaining the risk (e.g. “I understand all my data may get lost”. This is a well established pattern in registration forms, so we can expect users to understand what it conveys.
*   **Ask the user to confirm they have taken precautions**: if there is any action that the user can take to avoid the actions becoming destructive (e.g. “I have recovery keys for all my encrypted disks”)

  

![image.png](images/fe08b5a6-5ab8-405d-9bc6-af473cd915dd.png)

### Non-blocking friction

Non-blocking callouts are ideal for **mildly problematic or irreversible, non-destructive** actions. Example: FIPS is an advanced option in Ubuntu Pro that facilitates compliance with government standards, so it’s only useful for a minority of users. Once enabled, it cannot be disabled and Livepatch is permanently disabled.

​

In these cases, you may:

*   **Add a dialog to confirm the action**: It will add one extra step to the process, and the distinctive design will help grab the user's attention, but it will only add one extra click.

  

![image.png](images/35938308-6c6c-417c-87a9-3e0a788fe5d0.png)

​

*   **Show a warning message**: May be good enough for irreversible, non-destructive actions
*   *   Use [Notification](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-LYZ4XyOt-O) in Vanilla or warning info panel in Yaru.

  

![image.png](images/c52e9d6a-7fb6-4cce-b1a7-cf5360924434.png)

​You may also rely on **obscurity**. If an action is only supposed to be used by advanced users that know what they are doing, or in very specific circumstances, you may choose to make the option less visible, or to use technical language that will discourage users from playing with it. This will force users to be more deliberate, perhaps by relying on fully-fledged documentation that can provide full context.

i-N9wKI9QSCL.name: Form placement
i-N9wKI9QSCL.description: Form placement refers to the deliberate choice of surface: page, side panel, or modal, for presenting forms and multi-input tasks. The right surface depends on the complexity of the task, the amount of context the user needs from the current screen, and the level of disruption that is acceptable.
i-N9wKI9QSCL.content:
### Quick reference

Copy of Table 1

### Pages

Use a dedicated page when the form involves a complex, multi-step process that requires significant user attention and multiple [Input](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-9t3OC1fOBH) or interactions. Pages are appropriate when the content cannot be comfortably contained within a side panel, and when the user does not need to retain context from the previous screen. For example, creating or managing an item independently of where it will eventually appear.

### Side panels

Use a side panel when maintaining context from the current page is important. If the user is performing a task directly related to the content or structure of the current page, such as creating a sub-item or editing an inline component. The side panel allows them to maintain that relationship without disruption. Side panels work best for lightweight or medium-complexity forms where having the surrounding information visible enhances the user's understanding or efficiency.

### Modal windows

Modal windows should generally not be used for forms. Since modals interrupt user flow and demand focus, they are better suited for confirmation dialogs, warnings, or critical decisions, particularly destructive actions that may involve a confirmation [Input](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-9t3OC1fOBH) (such as typing "DELETE" to confirm an irreversible operation). Modals should not be used as a shortcut for tasks that are better suited to a full page or a contextual side panel.
